Journal.Title: The Journal of hand surgery, European volume

Abstract.AbstractText: Achieving balance in tendon repair is essential for restoring optimal function after tendon injuries, particularly in the hand and upper limb. We review and discuss key steps of 'balanced' flexor and extensor tendon repair and rehabilitation.Flexor and extensor tendon repair:To achieve optimal outcomes, the surgeon needs to consider the balance between mechanical strength and tendon gliding. Strong multi-strand repairs provide security against gapping and rupture, but overly tight or running epitenon sutures may impair synovial and blood supply to epitenon. Pulley venting in zone 2 is another balance: too little venting risks the repair catching or rupturing, while too much venting risks tendon bowstringing. In a wide-awake setting, intraoperative excursion testing shows the ideal venting length that allows a solid repair to glide freely without clinically significant bowstringing.In contaminated wounds, delayed repair decreases the risk of infection. Primary repairs are possible 1-2 weeks after injury, and even very late repairs can succeed if strong multi-strand core sutures are used and tension is carefully balanced. Repair tension should be sufficient to ensure that tendon ends are in close contact with slight bulkiness at the repair site to ensure a solid repair that allows early active digital motion. Extensor tendon injuries proximal to the fingers also require balance between protection and movement.

After flexor tendon repair, partial range early active flexion exercise is critical to maintain glide without rupture. After extensor tendon repair in the hand, relative motion splinting with strong repairs allows safer functional use or early active motion exercise. Patient education further help maintain glide without rupture.

Successful treatment requires a balance of various aspects of the repair process, including anatomical prerequisites, surgical techniques and rehabilitation strategies, through a multifaceted approach that encompasses careful surgical planning, accurate surgical repairs, optimal therapy protocol design and patient engagement.

AffiliationInfo.Affiliation: Department of Hand Surgery, Affiliated Hospital of Nantong University, Jiangsu, China.
Division of Plastic Surgery, Dalhousie University, Saint John, Canada.
